channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Kena: Bridge of Spirits Switch 2 Version Confirmed for Spring
item.content:encoded:
Developer and publisher Ember Lab has announced that its cute action-adventure game Kena: Bridge of Spirits is coming to the Switch 2 sometime this spring, and it'll include everything added as part of the original's Anniversary update as well.

In a message on social media (as well as a new trailer showing off the game on Nintendo's hardware), Ember Lab says Kena: Bridge of Spirits will arrive on Switch 2 sometime in the next couple of months, although there's no more specific release date than that, unfortunately.

When the game does launch on Switch 2, it'll include the new outfits, Charmstones, and Spirit Guide Trials introduced as part of 2022's Anniversary update, as well as New Game Plus mode, which will let you start Kena: Bridge of Spirits all over again with all of your abilities.

If you're not familiar with Kena: Bridge of Spirits, it's an action-adventure game originally released for both PS5 and PS4 back in 2021. The game casts you as the titular Kena, a spirit guide who must purge a forest of encroaching corruption.

In order to do so, she enlists the aid of the Rot, which, despite their name, are adorable little creatures that can assist Kena with combat and puzzle-solving. You can also collect little hats for them, which should spark joy in all but the most flinty of hearts.

Broadly speaking, Kena: Bridge of Spirits takes on a similar structure to games like The Legend of Zelda or Okami, although some of its combat encounters arguably owe more to the likes of the Souls series.

Developer Ember Lab isn't just working on the Switch 2 port of this game; the studio also recently announced a followup, Kena: Scars of Kosmora, and that one's only launching on PS5 and Steam (for now, at least).

Kena: Bridge of Spirits hits Switch 2 sometime this spring, and the game is also currently available on PC, PlayStation, and Xbox. Stay tuned for more.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/kena-bridge-of-spirits-switch-2-confirmed
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: MindsEye Dev Build a Rocket Boy Blames Further Layoffs on "Organized Espionage"
item.content:encoded:
MindsEye developer Build a Rocket Boy (BARB) has engaged in another round of staff layoffs, and it's laying the blame squarely at the door of "organized espionage", a charge it's alleged before.

As spotted by Insider Gaming, the studio announced the layoffs via a LinkedIn post yesterday. BARB doesn't specify how many employees are being laid off, but the company does say the decision to let them go is "deeply painful", "brutal", and "heartbreaking".

In the following paragraphs, CEO Mark Gerhard says that the studio's leadership "take[s] responsibility for the outcomes of [its] projects and the decisions that follow", but the part of the statement that follows this sentence arguably does the complete opposite of that.

Gerhard goes on to say that Build a Rocket Boy has been "working with external partners and legal advisors" in order to investigate "criminal activity" that allegedly took place around MindsEye's launch.

He says the work has "taken far longer than expected", but that his investigation has turned up "overwhelming evidence of organized espionage and corporate sabotage" surrounding MindsEye. Of course, he can't show you this evidence because "this matter is moving towards prosecution".

This isn't the first time Gerhard and Build a Rocket Boy have alleged sabotage as the reason MindsEye flopped. Back in February, the executive alleged that several influencers, journalists, and Build a Rocket Boy employees had engaged in "criminal interference" orchestrated by UK-based platform Ritual Network.

Naturally, that was a charge stringently denied by Ritual Network, and it's hard to imagine how a platform geared towards influencer management could cause a game to crash and burn on the scale MindsEye did. Still, perhaps when (or if) this case makes it to court, we'll find out.

Sadly, these also aren't the first layoffs Build a Rocket Boy has put into effect since MindsEye's launch. In June last year, the company announced it would let over 100 employees go, with some of those laid off by the studio subsequently alleging a culture of heavy crunch and a lack of coherent direction on the game.

In the meantime, if you'd like to find out what influencers and journalists allegedly tried to destroy, you can check out MindsEye right now on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S. Stay tuned for more.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/mindseye-build-a-rocket-boy-more-layoffs-espionage
